text: When CBS sold advertising time in the 1950s, network executives described their product with unusual candor: they were not selling entertainment to audiences but audiences to advertisers. The commodity was human attention, rented by the half-hour. This logic, which media critic Dallas Smythe called the audience commodity, turned viewers into labor. Watching commercials was unpaid work that delivered purchasing-ready minds to corporations. The transaction was invisible because the audience believed it was a recipient of content, not the product being sold.
question: What did Dallas Smythe mean by calling television viewers an 'audience commodity'?

options.text: Viewers paid a subscription that made them valuable customers
Networks sold viewers' attention to advertisers, making audiences the product
Audiences were compensated for watching commercials in the form of free content
Television sets were commodities distributed by corporate sponsors
